worth_it_explanation: Buyer and seat controls decide which demand sources are allowed to bid on a publisher's inventory and which are held out, so they sit at the point where brand safety meets fill rate. A blocklist that grew by accident, or an allowlist that was set once and never revisited, can hold out demand that would have paid while a publisher believes the controls are doing useful work. The check is a review of lists the publisher already maintains in Equativ and any Prebid bidder configuration, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete list entry the publisher can keep or remove on purpose.
intro: Seat controls decide which buyers and bidders are allowed to compete for a publisher's impressions and which are excluded. On a sell-side platform such as Equativ, and in any Prebid setup wired alongside it, these controls take the form of buyer allowlists and blocklists, enabled or disabled bidder adapters, and advertiser-level blocks applied through the bid request. This subcategory is about whether those lists reflect deliberate decisions the publisher can explain rather than entries that accumulated over time. The checks confirm that each block exists for a stated reason and that the controls protect the inventory without holding out demand that would have paid.

audit_checks.title: Buyer and bidder lists are not maintained against a stated reason
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Buyer allowlists, blocklists, and enabled bidders shape which demand reaches the auction. When the lists are not reviewed against a stated reason, blocks added for a one-time incident stay in place after the cause is gone and demand that would now pay is held out, so the publisher cannot tell which entries still earn their place.

educate.base:
A sell-side platform gives a publisher more than one lever for deciding who is allowed to bid. Equativ maintains buyer-level controls that allow or hold out individual demand partners and advertisers, and a Prebid setup wired alongside it carries its own bidder configuration, where each bidder adapter is either enabled or left out and per-bidder behavior is set in the bidderSettings object. The two layers work together. A buyer can be welcome at the platform level and still never reach the auction because the matching bidder adapter was never turned on, or the reverse.

Each of these controls exists to serve a purpose. A buyer might be held out after its creatives broke a policy, after a brand-safety review flagged it, or after a direct relationship took over that demand elsewhere. A bidder is enabled because it brings demand worth the added latency. The audit question is not whether the lists exist. It is whether each entry still maps to a reason the publisher can state today, or whether the lists have drifted from the decisions that created them.

Lists drift in predictable ways. A buyer blocked during a one-time creative incident stays blocked after the creative is long gone. A bidder enabled for a test never gets turned off once the test ends. An allowlist set up at launch is treated as settled and never revisited as new demand partners come online. None of these is a configuration error in the strict sense. Each is a decision that was correct once and was never re-examined, and the cost is demand held out or latency carried for no current reason.

A few signs point to lists that are not maintained against a stated reason:
  • Buyer blocklist or allowlist entries exist that no one on the team can tie to a current reason
  • Enabled bidder adapters include ones added for a test or a partner relationship that has since ended
  • No record exists of when each list was last reviewed or why entries were added
  • Advertiser-domain blocks set through the bid request duplicate or contradict the platform-level buyer controls
You may want to confirm that each buyer block, allowlist entry, and enabled bidder maps to a reason the publisher can state. One approach is to walk the lists with the publisher, record the reason for each entry, and remove the ones whose cause no longer holds.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a brand-safety review or an editorial standard often drives advertiser blocks, and those decisions can outlive the article or campaign that prompted them. You may want to record which blocks come from a standing editorial policy and which came from a one-time incident, so the standing ones stay and the incident-driven ones get a second look once the cause has passed.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, blocks on direct competitors or on advertisers that conflict with retail partnerships can be deliberate and worth keeping. You may want to confirm that each competitor block reflects a current partnership or policy rather than an old relationship, so the list does not hold out demand tied to a deal that has since ended.

investigate.intro: Review the buyer allowlists and blocklists in Equativ, the enabled bidder adapters in any Prebid configuration, and record the stated reason for each entry.

prebid_config.label: Prebid configuration review
steps: Open the Prebid configuration on the page and list the bidder adapters that are enabled.
For each enabled bidder, note any per-bidder behavior set in the bidderSettings object and whether the bidder is still needed.
Record any bidder that was added for a test or a partner relationship and check whether that reason still holds.

manual_inspection.label: Manual list review with the publisher
steps: List the buyer allowlist and blocklist entries maintained in the Equativ console.
For each entry, ask the publisher for the reason it was added and the date if known, and record whether the reason still applies.
Compare the platform-level buyer controls against any advertiser-domain blocks set through the bid request, and note any entry that duplicates or contradicts another.
Flag every entry that no one can tie to a current reason for a decision to keep or remove it on purpose.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each buyer allowlist entry, blocklist entry, and enabled bidder alongside the stated reason and, where known, the date it was added. A set of lists where every entry maps to a reason the publisher can state today passes. A set where entries exist with no current reason, or where test-era bidders remain enabled, or where platform-level and bid-request blocks contradict each other, is a fail recorded with the specific entries that lack a current reason.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each buyer and bidder list entry and its stated reason above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} maintains buyer and bidder lists in Equativ and its Prebid setup where some entries no longer map to a reason the team can state. Blocks added for a one-time incident, and bidders enabled for a test that has since ended, stay in place and hold out demand or carry latency for no current reason. {{voice_recommend}} walking the buyer allowlists, blocklists, and enabled bidder adapters with the publisher, recording the reason for each entry, and removing the ones whose cause no longer holds. After the review, keep a short record of when each list was last checked so the lists stay tied to decisions the publisher can explain.

audit_checks.title: Blocks are broad enough to suppress legitimate demand and fill
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block that reaches wider than intended removes legitimate demand from the auction along with the demand it was meant to exclude. Fewer bidders competing means a lower clearing price and unsold impressions, so an over-broad block trades fill and revenue for a protection the publisher did not need that wide.

educate.base:
Buyer and advertiser blocks protect a publisher's inventory, and the protection has a cost. Every block removes demand from the auction. When a block reaches only the demand it was meant to exclude, that cost is the price of the protection and worth paying. When a block reaches wider than intended, it removes legitimate demand along with the rest, and fewer bidders competing means a lower clearing price and impressions that go unsold.

Blocks reach too wide in a few ways. An advertiser-category block applied through the bid request can exclude a broad content category when the concern was a narrow slice of it. A blocklist that names a parent company can hold out every subsidiary, including ones the publisher would happily accept. Enforcing blocks on bids that simply lack the data to prove they are clean, rather than only on bids that fail a check, drops demand that may have been fine. The Prebid ORTB2 Blocking module makes this distinction explicit, separating the choice to enforce a block from the choice to enforce on unknown values, so a publisher can see which setting is removing which demand.

The way to tell a precise block from an over-broad one is to read what the block costs. A block tied to a real brand-safety or policy concern, scoped to the demand that raised it, shows up as a small dip in eligible demand and little change in fill. A block that reaches wider shows up as a drop in the number of bidders competing and a fall in fill or clearing price on the affected inventory, beyond what the named concern would explain.

A few signs point to blocks that reach wider than intended:
  • Advertiser-category or domain blocks exclude a broad category when the concern was a narrow part of it
  • Fill rate or the number of competing bidders drops on inventory covered by a block, beyond what the stated concern accounts for
  • Blocks enforce on bids missing data, e.g., a bid with no advertiser domain, rather than only on bids that fail a check
  • A blocklist entry names a parent company or a wide identifier that sweeps in demand the publisher would accept
You may want to confirm that each block is scoped to the demand it was meant to exclude rather than a wider set. One approach is to read fill rate and competing-bidder counts on the affected inventory before and after narrowing a block to the specific advertiser or category that raised the concern.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a brand-safety concern about a single advertiser can lead to a category-wide block that holds out demand the site would otherwise welcome. You may want to scope the block to the specific advertiser or the narrow content category that raised the concern, then read fill on the affected sections to confirm the narrower block kept the protection without the revenue loss.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a block meant to keep one competitor off product pages can sweep in an entire retail category if it is set too wide. You may want to confirm the block names the specific competing advertisers rather than the whole category, so demand from non-competing advertisers in that category still reaches the auction on the commercial inventory.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server ORTB2 Blocking Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed configuration documentation for buyer and advertiser blocks is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the scope of each buyer and advertiser block and read the fill rate and competing-bidder counts on the inventory each block covers.

prebid_config.label: Prebid configuration review
steps: Open the Prebid configuration and locate any advertiser-domain or category blocks set through the ORTB2 fields or a blocking module.
For each block, note whether it enforces only on bids that fail a check or also on bids that lack the data to prove they are clean.
Record the scope of each block, e.g., a single advertiser domain versus a broad category, and whether it is wider than the stated concern.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory each block covers, with fill rate, the number of competing bidders, and average clearing price.
Compare the affected inventory against comparable inventory with no block to see how much demand the block removed.
Note any case where fill or competing-bidder counts fall further than the named concern would explain, which points to a block reaching wider than intended.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each buyer and advertiser block with its scope and the fill rate and competing-bidder counts on the inventory it covers. A set of blocks each scoped to the demand it was meant to exclude, with fill and competing-bidder counts holding steady beyond the named exclusion, passes. A block that excludes a broad category for a narrow concern, or that enforces on bids missing data, or that coincides with a drop in fill or bidder counts beyond the stated reason, is a fail recorded with the affected inventory and the demand the block removed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block's scope and the fill rate and competing-bidder counts on the inventory it covers above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} carries buyer or advertiser blocks that reach wider than the concern that prompted them, so legitimate demand is held out alongside the demand the block was meant to exclude. Fewer bidders competing on the affected inventory means a lower clearing price and impressions that go unsold, a cost beyond the protection the publisher intended. {{voice_recommend}} scoping each block to the specific advertiser or narrow category that raised the concern, and, where a block enforces on bids missing data, limiting it to bids that fail a check rather than bids that simply lack it. After narrowing each block, read fill rate and competing-bidder counts on the affected inventory to confirm the protection held without the revenue loss.
